title: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abortion's Immediate Abolition
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman documents how Oklahomans for Life (OFL), the state affiliate of National Right to Life, uses its 2020 candidate questionnaire to extract pledges from candidates that they will oppose the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act and accept compromise exceptions like rape and incest. The article argues OFL's endorsement is a reliable signal that a candidate will block the immediate abolition of abortion.
audiences: Pro-life voters in Oklahoma evaluating candidate endorsements
Pro-life lobbyists and National Right to Life affiliates
Christian voters wrestling with compromise versus principle
State legislators considering whether to seek OFL's endorsement

questions.question: How long has Oklahomans for Life been the primary organizational opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act?
questions.answer: Since the bill was first introduced in 2016. OFL has supplied senators with anti-abolition talking points and devised strategies to deny the bill a hearing, with abolitionists obtaining copies of OFL strategy letters in both 2016 and 2019.
questions.quote: Oklahomans for Life (OFL) has been the primary opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act since the bill was first introduced in 2016. They’ve given Senators anti-abolition talking points and provided strategies for denying the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act a hearing. Abolitionists were able to obtain copies of OFL’s anti-abolition strategy letters in 2016 and 2019.

questions.question: What does Question 3 of OFL's 2020 candidate questionnaire actually ask candidates to commit to?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters in Oklahoma evaluating candidate endorsements
questions.answer: It asks candidates to pledge they will oppose attempts to repeal existing pro-life laws, even by groups that purport to oppose abortion, unless those efforts are part of a comprehensive restructuring approved by Oklahomans for Life. In context, this is a direct request to oppose the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.
questions.quote: Question 3 of the Oklahomans for Life 2020 candidate questionnaire requests candidates’ opposition to the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, by asking them whether they will **“oppose attempts to repeal existing pro-life laws, even by entities purporting to oppose abortion, except as part of a comprehensive restructuring of pro-life laws designed both to withstand court scrutiny and to save the lives of unborn children as supported by Oklahomans for Life.”**

questions.question: Why must an abolition bill repeal existing pro-life regulations to function?
questions.audience: Christian voters wrestling with compromise versus principle
questions.answer: Because regulatory pro-life laws presuppose that abortion remains legal so long as certain procedural conditions are satisfied. A statute declaring abortion legal-if-X cannot logically coexist with a statute declaring abortion illegal, so the regulations must be repealed for abolition to take effect.
questions.quote: A law establishing that abortion is legal as long as the mother is offered an ultrasound cannot coexist with a law establishing that abortion is illegal. A law establishing that abortion is legal as long as the baby is younger than 20 weeks cannot coexist with a law establishing that abortion is illegal. A law establishing that abortion is legal as long as the mother is made aware of the abortion reversal pill cannot coexist with a law establishing that abortion is illegal.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the structure of every existing pro-life regulation?
questions.answer: He argues every pro-life regulation establishes that abortion remains legal provided the abortionist or mother first complies with a procedural requirement. As long as such laws stand, abortion cannot be abolished, which is why repealing them is one of the five components of an abolition bill.
questions.quote: Every pro-life law establishes that abortion is legal as long as the abortionist and/or the mother jump through a pro-life hoop first. Abortion cannot be abolished while these laws remain on the books. A bill to abolish abortion must repeal them. This is why the repeal of these abortion-regulations is one of the [five components of an abolition bill]

questions.question: Who does the questionnaire's phrase about "entities purporting to oppose abortion" target, and how does Silberman respond?
questions.audience: Pro-life lobbyists and National Right to Life affiliates
questions.answer: It refers to Free the States and other state-level abolitionist organizations. Silberman counters that the label more accurately fits National Right to Life itself, which raises millions annually and uses some of that money to oppose efforts to immediately abolish abortion.
questions.quote: However, the question of whether an organization purports to oppose abortion or actually opposes abortion could rightly be asked of an organization like National Right to Life (of which OFL is a state-level affiliate) which brings in millions of dollars annually and uses part of that money to oppose efforts to immediately abolish abortion.

questions.question: What does Question 2 of the OFL questionnaire reveal about the organization's philosophy on exceptions?
questions.audience: Christian voters wrestling with compromise versus principle
questions.answer: Question 2 asks candidates whether, if a no-exceptions ban fails, they will vote for a fallback law allowing abortion in cases of life of the mother, forcible rape reported within seven days, or incest against a minor reported to authorities. Silberman argues this shows compromise is central to OFL's and National Right to Life's approach.
questions.quote: “If efforts to enact such a law [to prohibit abortion upon reversal of *Roe v. Wade* except to prevent the death of the mother] failed, will you vote for a law that would prohibit abortion except when necessary to prevent the death of the mother, or when the pregnancy is the result of forcible rape (reported to law enforcement authorities within seven days), or when the pregnancy resulted from an act of incest committed against a minor (with the perpetrator reported to law enforcement authorities)?”

questions.question: Why does Silberman say a rape exception is unnecessary in Oklahoma specifically?
questions.audience: State legislators considering whether to seek OFL's endorsement
questions.answer: Because pro-lifers control every lever of state government in Oklahoma, there is no political need to compromise. A firm line could pass a no-exceptions ban, but OFL prepares candidates to compromise their principles from the outset.
questions.quote: In a state like Oklahoma, there’s no need to compromise and include the rape exception. Pro-lifers control everything. If you really wanted to enact a law to prohibit abortion upon reversal of *Roe v. Wade,* what you have to do is hold a firm line. Instead, OFL works to prepare candidates to compromise their principles from the get-go.

questions.question: What practical voter guidance does the article offer regarding OFL's endorsement?
questions.audience: Pro-life voters in Oklahoma evaluating candidate endorsements
questions.answer: Silberman treats an OFL endorsement as a reliable indicator that a candidate will oppose abortion's immediate abolition, and urges both candidates and voters to treat the endorsement as a liability rather than an asset.
questions.quote: Oklahomans for Life’s endorsement is a good way to know a candidate is going to oppose abortion’s immediate abolition. Candidates should not want OFL’s endorsement and Oklahomans should not want the candidates they support to have OFL’s endorsement.

related.title: Uncovered Letter Exposes Plan to Keep Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Provides the underlying 2019 Tony Lauinger strategy letter that this article cites as evidence of OFL's organized opposition.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Earlier Silberman piece naming Tony Lauinger and other figures responsible for blocking the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13"?
related.reason: Directly hyperlinked from this article to explain why the abolition bill must repeal existing pro-life regulations.

related.title: National Right to Life Endorses Greg McCortney, the Senator Who Kept Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Concrete example of the same 2020 election cycle where OFL's parent organization endorsed a senator who blocked abolition.

related.title: Here's What We're Asking Oklahoma Candidates
related.reason: Companion piece presenting Free the States' own candidate questionnaire as a counter to OFL's compromise-oriented survey.

related.title: Never Be Impressed By a Politician's Claim to Be "100% Pro-Life"
related.reason: Reinforces the article's thesis that pro-life endorsements and self-labels are unreliable signals on abolition.

related.title: National Right to Life is Pro-Abortion: Oklahoma SB 834
related.reason: Later Silberman article extending the same critique of NRTL/OFL pushing legislation that preserves rather than abolishes abortion.
